Advisor: When you say “no dependents,” is that zero—0—on your end, David?
Client 2: Yes, 0.
Advisor: Perfect. And marital status—just so I reflect it correctly in the plan—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, married.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to connect the dots with what we covered: income, assets, no liabilities, and Emily’s life insurance—those are the big buckets we’ve captured today.

Advisor: Great, thank you. And when we’re thinking about taxes, what bracket do you generally assume you’re in—just the band you plan around?
Client 2: I always forget if that’s the 32% one or—
Client 1: It’s 35%.
Advisor: Got it—35%. Thanks for the save, Emily.
Advisor: And then risk-wise—when markets get choppy, how would you describe your comfort level? Conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits best?

Client 1: Moderate. We’re preservation-leaning, but it’s still moderate.
Advisor: Okay, moderate—makes sense.
Advisor: Last one is more about how you want to work together. Are you looking for an RIA (registered investment advisor) relationship—ongoing advice and management—or more of a one-time plan?
Client 1: We want RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. And just so I’m aligned, Emily—your preference is still clear downside protection first, then growth, right?

Client 1: It’s 35%.

Client 2: Yes, retired.
Advisor: Great. Now, Emily, I know we talked through the income sources—pensions and then interest and dividends—but I want to sanity-check the total we’re using for you personally. What’s your gross annual income number that you want me to plan around?

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that 143400 is your gross number before taxes, just so I’m not mixing net and gross.
Client 2: Yeah, before taxes—gross. I always mix those up.
